raw_response: {"rating": 4.5, "lean": "Right", "explanation": "This is an Opinion/Editorial that consistently advances a culturally conservative critique of higher education. The author derides progressive-oriented coursework and campus culture, elevates traditional Western canon and law-and-order values, and provides no balancing perspectives, yielding a Right rating. The one-sided framing and mocking tone throughout support a +4.5 classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) Loaded, mocking language frames universities and courses as wasteful or absurd, e.g., calling Ivy offerings “ridiculous,” labeling curricula “nonsensical progressive orthodoxy” and “intersectionality-laden,” and saying schools are “high on their own supply”; (2) Law-and-order framing critical of leniency toward theft, asserting New Yorkers “don’t even bat an eye” at shoplifting and suggesting a lost “sense of right and wrong,” aligns with right-leaning criminal-justice views; (3) Defense of tradition and Western canon: the author argues that colleges have “lost sight of their mission,” insists “Young people need a grasp of the texts and ideas that make Western civilization great,” and laments sidelining classics; (4) Selection bias in examples: the piece spotlights culture-war courses (intersectionality, queer/disability studies, pop culture like video games and music) at elite institutions to portray academia as impractical and ideological, with no inclusion of counterexamples or defenders; (5) Fiscal/ROI skepticism: repeated emphasis on “eye-watering” tuition, imagined six-figure debts, and doubts about workplace relevance reflects a fiscally conservative critique of higher ed; (6) Rhetorical questions and ridicule reinforce a singular viewpoint (“Whatever happened to ‘The Odyssey’?”; “How is an employer supposed to know whether you read the classics… or played video games…”) without presenting alternative arguments; (7) Data is used selectively (polls showing declining confidence and a syllabi analysis) to buttress skepticism, with no countervailing data or methodological caveats, amplifying the one-directional framing."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is an Opinion/Editorial that consistently advances a culturally conservative critique of higher education. The author derides progressive-oriented coursework and campus culture, elevates traditional Western canon and law-and-order values, and provides no balancing perspectives, yielding a Right rating. The one-sided framing and mocking tone throughout support a +4.5 classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) Loaded, mocking language frames universities and courses as wasteful or absurd, e.g., calling Ivy offerings “ridiculous,” labeling curricula “nonsensical progressive orthodoxy” and “intersectionality-laden,” and saying schools are “high on their own supply”; (2) Law-and-order framing critical of leniency toward theft, asserting New Yorkers “don’t even bat an eye” at shoplifting and suggesting a lost “sense of right and wrong,” aligns with right-leaning criminal-justice views; (3) Defense of tradition and Western canon: the author argues that colleges have “lost sight of their mission,” insists “Young people need a grasp of the texts and ideas that make Western civilization great,” and laments sidelining classics; (4) Selection bias in examples: the piece spotlights culture-war courses (intersectionality, queer/disability studies, pop culture like video games and music) at elite institutions to portray academia as impractical and ideological, with no inclusion of counterexamples or defenders; (5) Fiscal/ROI skepticism: repeated emphasis on “eye-watering” tuition, imagined six-figure debts, and doubts about workplace relevance reflects a fiscally conservative critique of higher ed; (6) Rhetorical questions and ridicule reinforce a singular viewpoint (“Whatever happened to ‘The Odyssey’?”; “How is an employer supposed to know whether you read the classics… or played video games…”) without presenting alternative arguments; (7) Data is used selectively (polls showing declining confidence and a syllabi analysis) to buttress skepticism, with no countervailing data or methodological caveats, amplifying the one-directional framing.

explanation: This is an Opinion/Editorial that consistently advances a culturally conservative critique of higher education. The author derides progressive-oriented coursework and campus culture, elevates traditional Western canon and law-and-order values, and provides no balancing perspectives, yielding a Right rating. The one-sided framing and mocking tone throughout support a +4.5 classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) Loaded, mocking language frames universities and courses as wasteful or absurd, e.g., calling Ivy offerings “ridiculous,” labeling curricula “nonsensical progressive orthodoxy” and “intersectionality-laden,” and saying schools are “high on their own supply”; (2) Law-and-order framing critical of leniency toward theft, asserting New Yorkers “don’t even bat an eye” at shoplifting and suggesting a lost “sense of right and wrong,” aligns with right-leaning criminal-justice views; (3) Defense of tradition and Western canon: the author argues that colleges have “lost sight of their mission,” insists “Young people need a grasp of the texts and ideas that make Western civilization great,” and laments sidelining classics; (4) Selection bias in examples: the piece spotlights culture-war courses (intersectionality, queer/disability studies, pop culture like video games and music) at elite institutions to portray academia as impractical and ideological, with no inclusion of counterexamples or defenders; (5) Fiscal/ROI skepticism: repeated emphasis on “eye-watering” tuition, imagined six-figure debts, and doubts about workplace relevance reflects a fiscally conservative critique of higher ed; (6) Rhetorical questions and ridicule reinforce a singular viewpoint (“Whatever happened to ‘The Odyssey’?”; “How is an employer supposed to know whether you read the classics… or played video games…”) without presenting alternative arguments; (7) Data is used selectively (polls showing declining confidence and a syllabi analysis) to buttress skepticism, with no countervailing data or methodological caveats, amplifying the one-directional framing.
